intro: Starbucks calls its five-tier coffeehouse career ladder "Craft Your Path." Every level below is drawn from Starbucks' own official role descriptions and job postings — not a third party's guess at what the job involves.

ladder.title: Barista
ladder.summary: Baristas are the heart of Starbucks — skilled in connecting and creating moments, and an expert in handcrafting delicious beverages, working together to create a welcoming coffeehouse environment.
responsibilities: Greet and connect with every customer
Handcraft beverages to recipe and quality standards
Operate the register and handle transactions
Rotate between bar, register, and food stations
Maintain a clean, stocked, food-safe store
Support mobile order, pickup, and delivery fulfillment
ladder.requirements: Entry-level — no prior experience required. Most weeks run 20–40 hours, part-time or full-time.

pay.note: Starbucks' own minimum starting wage commitment for U.S. company-operated stores; total compensation value (wages plus benefits) averages roughly $30/hour for partners working 20+ hours/week.

ladder.pathToNext: About 6–12 months of strong performance, reliable availability (often 25+ hrs/week), and demonstrated leadership on shift are typically what managers look for before recommending someone for Shift Supervisor.

ladder.title: Shift Supervisor
ladder.summary: Shift Supervisors run shifts, lead the coffeehouse team, and make decisions that impact operations — a first step into leadership and expanded responsibility.
responsibilities: Execute store operations during scheduled shifts
Deploy partners and delegate tasks across the floor
Coach and positively reinforce new-partner training
Give the store manager feedback on partner performance
Spot morale or performance shifts and flag them to the manager
Uphold recipe, presentation, and service standards
ladder.requirements: Typically requires several months of barista experience and flexible availability; formal Shift Supervisor training program required after selection.

pay.note: Cash wage estimated from crowdsourced pay data (Indeed, ZipRecruiter, Glassdoor); varies by market — some regional reports run notably higher.

ladder.pathToNext: Assistant Store Manager openings usually go to shift supervisors who've shown they can run a shift independently and are ready to take on staffing, scheduling, and business-result ownership.

ladder.title: Assistant Store Manager
ladder.summary: Assistant Managers inspire customers while developing management skills on their journey to running a coffeehouse of their own — managing operations, driving business results, leading a team, and developing talent.
responsibilities: Co-manage daily store operations with the Store Manager
Support hiring, scheduling, and partner development
Help drive sales, labor, and cost targets
Step in as the store's leader in the manager's absence
Coach shift supervisors and baristas on standards
ladder.requirements: Usually promoted from Shift Supervisor; salaried position with full management accountability.

pay.note: Salaried estimate synthesized from ZipRecruiter, Indeed, and Comparably; higher in metro markets like New York or California, lower in lower-cost regions.

ladder.pathToNext: Most Assistant Store Managers move up to Store Manager within roughly 1–3 years, based on performance and store/business needs.

ladder.title: Store Manager
ladder.summary: Store Managers run their coffeehouse as if it belongs to them — from daily operations to growing the business — spending time on the floor to connect with employees and customers, coach in the moment, and drive results.
responsibilities: Own the store's full P&L and business performance
Hire, develop, and build the store's leadership bench
Set the store's operational and service standards
Drive community connection and local marketing
Coach Assistant Managers and Shift Supervisors directly
ladder.requirements: Almost always promoted internally; Starbucks aims to grow the large majority of its leaders from within rather than hiring store leadership externally.

pay.note: Base salary plus an annual performance bonus (roughly $5,000–$20,000 depending on store results) and Bean Stock equity; range synthesized from Glassdoor, ZipRecruiter, and PayScale, which vary widely by market and store volume.

ladder.pathToNext: District Manager roles typically require 5+ years of multi-unit retail leadership — managing P&L, inventory, operations, and customer service across more than one location — so Store Managers usually spend several years proving results before moving up.

ladder.title: District Manager
ladder.summary: District Managers lead a multi-coffeehouse portfolio and are accountable for performance across their district — assessing the business, building plans to drive results, and coaching the next generation of store leaders.
responsibilities: Oversee performance across a portfolio of coffeehouses
Coach and develop Store Managers and future leaders
Translate company strategy into district-level plans
Build relationships with the district's local communities
Own hiring and succession planning for store leadership
ladder.requirements: 5+ years of retail experience managing multiple stores, departments, or units, including P&L, inventory, and operations — usually reached by promotion from Store Manager.

pay.note: Synthesized from multiple 2026 salary-aggregator reports (ZipRecruiter, Indeed, and others), which cluster in the low-$100Ks to mid-$100Ks depending on district size and region.

ladder.pathToNext: Beyond District Manager, Starbucks' regional and corporate retail-operations leadership roles (Regional Director and above) draw heavily from this same internal pipeline.

external.label: New to Starbucks
steps: Explore open roles on the Career Hub and read the job description closely — it lists exactly what the role expects and offers.
Create a profile, upload a resume, and turn on job alerts for the role and location you want.
Click Apply and submit your application online.
Watch for an email confirmation, then track status through the Career Hub — a recruiter or coffeehouse leader reaches out directly if you're selected.

internal.label: Current partners (promotion track)
internal.description: Existing partners use the same Career Hub, signed in through the partner portal, to see and apply for internal-only postings — this is the normal route from Barista into Shift Supervisor and beyond. Many partners also start by simply telling their store manager they're interested; managers can recommend strong partners for the next level's training program directly.

tips: Lead your resume with a clear headline and quantify impact from your current role — don't just restate the job description.
Connect your experience directly to the priorities listed in the job posting you're applying to.
List any Starbucks trainings, certifications, or recognitions you've completed if you're already a partner.
Prepare specific, story-based examples for behavioral interview questions, plus 3–5 questions of your own for the interviewer.
Send a thank-you note within 48 hours of an interview.
howToApply.trainingMilestones: New partners get essential training in their first month, with additional structured training checkpoints at 90 days and 190 days — the same on-ramp used before any leadership training program begins.
